# Common Disasters and Recovery

This is the chapter to open when something has just gone wrong. It is a
panic reference: find your symptom, run the fix, and read the
explanation later. Every command block was run for real against a
throwaway clone of the running example; the output is real, lightly
normalized only where a carriage-return progress bar would otherwise
smear across the page. Your hashes will differ.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
Before you touch anything, three rules that prevent most disasters from
becoming permanent:

1. **Do not force-push, and do not delete the repository.** Almost
   everything local is recoverable; a panicked `push --force` over good
   work on the remote often is not.
2. **`git reflog` is your undo history.** Git records every position
   `HEAD` has held. Reachable entries are kept 90 days by default;
   commits orphaned by a reset or rebase are unreachable and expire in
   30 (`gc.reflogExpireUnreachable`). That is still weeks, so lost
   commits are usually still there, but recover sooner rather than later.
3. **The dividing line is whether you have pushed.** If a bad commit is
   still only on your machine, you may safely rewrite it (`reset`,
   `--amend`, `rebase`). Once it is pushed and shared, undo it with a
   *new* commit (`revert`) instead of rewriting shared history.
:::

## Symptom-to-fix table {.unnumbered}

| Symptom | Fix | Section |
|---|---|---|
| Edited a file, want the committed version back | `git restore <file>` | [1](#sec-restore) |
| Staged something by mistake | `git restore --staged <file>` | [2](#sec-unstage) |
| Committed on the wrong branch (not pushed) | `git branch X` then `git reset --hard HEAD~1` | [3](#sec-wrongbranch) |
| Last commit included a file it should not (not pushed) | `git rm --cached` then `git commit --amend` | [4](#sec-amend) |
| Need to undo a commit that is already pushed | `git revert <hash>` | [5](#sec-revert) |
| Lost commits to a reset or bad rebase | `git reflog` then `git reset --hard` | [6](#sec-reflog) |
| Made commits on a detached HEAD | `git switch -c <name>` | [7](#sec-detached) |
| A merge is going wrong | `git merge --abort` | [8](#sec-mergeabort) |
| A secret is buried in history | rotate it, then `git filter-repo` | [9](#sec-secret) |

## 1. Restore a file to its committed version {#sec-restore .unnumbered}

You edited a file, the edit is wrong, and you want the last committed
version back. `git restore` overwrites the working copy from the repo.

```bash
$ git status --short
 M README.md
$ git restore README.md
$ git status --short
```

The clean `git status` confirms the file is back to its committed state.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
`git restore <file>` permanently discards uncommitted changes to that
file; they were never committed, so the reflog cannot bring them back.
This is the one common operation that is genuinely irreversible. Be sure
you want the edit gone before you run it.
:::

## 2. Unstage a file you added by mistake {#sec-unstage .unnumbered}

You ran `git add` (often the reflexive `git add .`) and pulled in
something that should not be staged. `git restore --staged` removes it
from the staging area while leaving the file on disk untouched.

```bash
$ git add .
$ git status --short
A  data_dump.csv
$ git restore --staged data_dump.csv
$ git status --short
?? data_dump.csv
```

The file went from staged (`A`) back to untracked (`??`): unstaged, but
not deleted.

## 3. Move a commit off the wrong branch {#sec-wrongbranch .unnumbered}

You committed on `main` but the work belonged on a feature branch, and
you have not pushed. Create a branch at the current commit (which keeps
it), then rewind `main` by one.

```bash
$ git log --oneline -2
51ea46e Try an experimental specification
dcb18de Note headline result in README
$ git branch experiment
$ git reset --hard HEAD~1
HEAD is now at dcb18de Note headline result in README
$ git log --oneline -2 experiment
51ea46e Try an experimental specification
dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

`main` is back where it should be, and the commit now lives on
`experiment`, ready to keep building on.

## 4. Drop a file from your last commit {#sec-amend .unnumbered}

Your most recent commit included a large data file (or any wrong file)
and you have not pushed. Untrack the file, ignore it, and amend the
*same* commit so the mistake never appears in history.

```bash
$ git show --stat --oneline HEAD | head -5
6f53f11 Add cleaning tweak (oops, also staged the raw CSV)
 data/raw_panel.csv  | Bin 0 -> 2000000 bytes
 scripts/01_clean.py |   2 ++
 2 files changed, 2 insertions(+)
$ git rm --cached data/raw_panel.csv
rm 'data/raw_panel.csv'
$ echo 'data/' >> .gitignore
$ git add .gitignore
$ git commit --amend --no-edit -q
$ git show --stat --oneline HEAD | head -5
da219e5 Add cleaning tweak (oops, also staged the raw CSV)
 .gitignore          | 1 +
 scripts/01_clean.py | 2 ++
 2 files changed, 3 insertions(+)
```

The amended commit (`da219e5`) keeps the real change and the new ignore
rule, and the data file is gone from it.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
`--amend` rewrites the last commit into a new one, so use it only if you
have **not pushed**. If the big file was committed several commits back,
or already pushed, `--amend` is not enough: that is the deeper rewrite
in Section 9. And if the file was a secret, ignoring it is never enough
on its own (Chapter 6): rotate it.
:::

## 5. Undo a commit that is already pushed {#sec-revert .unnumbered}

A bad commit is already on the shared remote. Do not rewrite history:
that breaks every collaborator's clone (Chapter 7). Instead, `git
revert` adds a new commit that is the exact inverse of the bad one.

```bash
$ git log --oneline -2
f835586 Record headline effect (WRONG)
dcb18de Note headline result in README
$ git revert --no-edit HEAD
[main d965d52] Revert "Record headline effect (WRONG)"
 Date: Thu Feb 12 09:31:00 2026 +0000
 1 file changed, 2 deletions(-)
$ git log --oneline -3
d965d52 Revert "Record headline effect (WRONG)"
f835586 Record headline effect (WRONG)
dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

The error is undone by a forward commit (`d965d52`); the original commit
stays in history, so nothing your collaborators have is invalidated.

## 6. Recover commits lost to a reset or bad rebase {#sec-reflog .unnumbered}

A careless `git reset --hard` (or a botched rebase, Chapter 7) appears
to have destroyed commits. It did not. The reflog still holds them.

```bash
$ git reset --hard HEAD~2
HEAD is now at dcb18de Note headline result in README
$ git reflog -4
dcb18de HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~2
2392ecf HEAD@{1}: commit: Add analysis B
67e52cc HEAD@{2}: commit: Add analysis A
dcb18de HEAD@{3}: checkout: moving from dcb18de... to main
$ git reset --hard HEAD@{1}
HEAD is now at 2392ecf Add analysis B
```

`HEAD@{1}` was the tip before the reset; resetting to it restores both
lost commits at once. (For a rebase specifically, `git reset --hard
ORIG_HEAD` does the same without reading the reflog.)

## 7. Rescue commits made on a detached HEAD {#sec-detached .unnumbered}

You checked out an old commit to look around, then committed. Those
commits belong to no branch and would be garbage-collected eventually.
Naming a branch where you are makes them permanent.

```bash
$ git switch --detach HEAD~1
HEAD is now at f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
$ git switch -c saved-experiment
Switched to a new branch 'saved-experiment'
$ git log --oneline -2
47cc466 Experiment from an old commit
f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
```

The experiment now lives on `saved-experiment`; it can no longer be lost
to garbage collection.

## 8. Back out of a merge in progress {#sec-mergeabort .unnumbered}

A merge hit conflicts and you would rather not resolve them now (wrong
branch, bad timing). `git merge --abort` returns the tree to exactly its
pre-merge state.

```bash
$ git merge --no-edit other
Auto-merging README.md
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in README.md
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.
$ git merge --abort
$ git status --short
$ git log --oneline -1
b10c4a1 Edit headline (main)
```

The clean `git status` and unchanged tip show the merge attempt left no
trace. (If a bad merge already *completed*, `git reset --hard
ORIG_HEAD` undoes it the same way, as long as you have not pushed.)

## 9. Purge a secret buried in history {#sec-secret .unnumbered}

A credentials file was committed and is now several commits deep. This
is the one case where you must rewrite history even though it is
painful, and the order of operations matters.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
**Step 0, before any Git command: rotate the secret.** Revoke the key or
change the password at its source. Once it is revoked, a copy left in
some old clone is harmless. Rewriting history removes the embarrassment;
rotating the key removes the danger. Chapter 6 covers why, and why
GitHub support cannot scrub it from every fork and cache for you.
:::

The secret is retrievable from history until you remove it, then
`git-filter-repo` purges it from every commit.

```bash
$ git log --oneline -- config/secrets.yaml
56f4637 Wire up vendor credentials
$ git show HEAD~1:config/secrets.yaml
vendor_api_key: "DEMO_KEY_NOT_A_SECRET"
$ git filter-repo --invert-paths \
      --path config/secrets.yaml --force
Parsed 6 commits
HEAD is now at 6f033d5 Continue analysis
New history written in 0.01 seconds; now repacking/cleaning...
Repacking your repo and cleaning out old unneeded objects
Completely finished after 0.02 seconds.
$ git log --oneline -- config/secrets.yaml
```

The final empty `git log` confirms the file exists in no commit. After
this you must force-push and have collaborators re-clone, and the secret
must already have been rotated (Step 0).

## The golden rules, restated {.unnumbered}

When the panic passes, these are the habits that turn most disasters
into minor detours: commit small and often so any lost work is small;
run `git reflog` before doing anything drastic, because it almost always
has your answer; never rewrite history you have already pushed, reaching
for `revert` instead of `reset` once work is shared; and treat a leaked
secret as a rotation problem first and a Git problem second. Prevention,
covered throughout this book, beats every recovery in this chapter.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Deliberately cause and fix each disaster in a throwaway clone, so the
recovery is muscle memory before you need it for real.

1. Make and discard an unstaged edit with `git restore`. Then stage a
   file and unstage it with `git restore --staged`. Note which one is
   reversible and which is not.
2. Commit something on `main`, then move it to a new branch with `git
   branch` plus `git reset --hard HEAD~1`. Confirm both branches with
   `git log`.
3. Commit a large dummy file by accident, then remove it from that
   commit with `git rm --cached` and `git commit --amend`.
4. Make two commits, `git reset --hard HEAD~2` to "lose" them, and
   recover them from `git reflog`. This is the single most valuable
   drill in the chapter.
5. Optional, and only in a throwaway repo: commit a fake secrets file,
   then purge it with `git filter-repo --invert-paths`. Write down, in
   order, what you would do first if the key were real.
